news_summary: New discoveries suggest that ancient Egyptian princesses buried with weapons may have been warriors, shedding light on their active roles in history.
news_summary_fr: De nouvelles découvertes laissent penser que les princesses de l'Égypte antique enterrées avec des armes auraient pu être des guerrières, ce qui met en lumière leur rôle actif dans l'histoire.
news_summary_es: Los nuevos descubrimientos sugieren que las princesas del Antiguo Egipto que fueron enterradas con armas podrían haber sido guerreras, lo que arroja luz sobre su papel activo en la historia.

articles.title: Ancient Egyptian princesses buried with weapons may have been fighters
articles.summary: The mummified daughters of pharaohs who lived thousands of years ago have bone changes that suggest they fought with bows and daggers
articles.content:
The mummified daughters of pharaohs who lived thousands of years ago have bone changes that suggest they fought with bows and daggers

The dagger buried with Princess Ita Sameh Abdel Mohsen

Ancient Egyptian princesses were probably trained in using bows and daggers and put themselves at risk of serious injury. Rediscovering mummies from about 3800 years ago has revealed that some of these women had enlarged bones in their arms and bowing of the bones in their palms, which suggests they were active users of the weapons they were often buried with.

“They carried their bows and daggers into the afterlife not just as ornaments, but as testaments to the active, resilient and powerful lives they led,” says Zeinab Hashesh at the University of Beni-Suef in Egypt.

Weapons have been found buried with women from many cultures throughout history. But there has been much debate about whether this was symbolic or meant the women used them.

Advertisement

Now, Hashesh and her colleagues have studied six royal mummies – five female – found at the Dahshur complex of pyramids and tombs from Egypt’s Middle Kingdom between about 1850 and 1700 BC. The mummies, some of which were found with bows and arrows alongside them, were excavated in the 1890s, and were rediscovered in the Egyptian Museum in Cairo during a curation project in 2020.

The team has analysed the bones to learn about the lives of these royal individuals, four of whom are thought to be daughters of the pharaoh Amenemhat II.

A key finding was that Princess Ita – who had an elaborate gold and lapis lazuli dagger in her burial assemblage and was aged between 28 and 34 when she died – would have had very strong connections between the bones in her forearm and strong hand muscles. This implies the habitual gripping of weapons like daggers or maces, says Hashesh.

The arrows buried with Princess Noub-Hotep Eman Shawky

Princesses Noub-Hotep (aged between 40 and 44) and Itaweret (aged 20 to 34) both showed enlargement of the radius bone in the forearm, which could be an adaptation to the repetitive stress of drawing a bow. The former princess, who was buried alongside arrows, also had bowing of the second right metacarpal bone in the palm and strengthened attachments of finger muscles. These hint at the sustained mechanical load required to hold a bow and are indicative of “archer’s grip”.

“I imagine the lives of princesses like Noub-Hotep and Ita were far from the sedentary, purely decorative existence we often associate with ancient royalty,” says Hashesh. “To develop the archer’s grip and the structural bowing of the hand bones observed in Noub-Hotep, they must have begun training in archery and martial arts from a young age.”

Michelle Langley at Griffith University in Australia says this study gives us real insight into these princesses’ lives. “Royal women were not simply sitting around a palace or following their menfolk around, but living active and skilled lives. They were trained in very practical martial and hunting arts, just as we imagine their fathers and brothers were,” she says.

The bones also revealed that injuries were common. For example, Princess Itaweret survived broken ribs and foot fractures, which Hashesh says were probably caused by accidents or hard blows.

“This tells us that they were out in the world, perhaps engaged in high-impact activities where falls and blows were a real danger,” she says. When injured, their status gave them access to surgeons capable of setting bones so well that they healed without infection or misalignment, she says.

But Sonia Zakrzewski at the University of Southampton, UK, says that other activities could result in similar alterations to arm and hand bones, such as frequent juggling or using a scythe. “If we were to find a tennis player buried with a club, we cannot simply argue that this [similar] bony change was a result of clubbing people,” she says.
articles.reasoning: The article reveals new scientific insights into the active, skilled lives of ancient Egyptian princesses, challenging traditional views of royal women as passive figures. This discovery has broad cultural and historical significance, enriching our understanding of gender roles in ancient societies and highlighting the resilience and agency of women in history.
articles.personality_title: Ancient Egyptian princesses likely trained as fighters, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Ancient Egyptian princesses were often buried with weapons like bows and daggers, but it was unclear if they actually used them or if the weapons were just symbols.

**What happened** – Researchers studied six royal mummies from about 3800 years ago, including five women believed to be daughters of Pharaoh Amenemhat II. They found changes in the princesses' arm and hand bones that suggest these women regularly used weapons. For example, Princess Ita had strong forearm bones linked to gripping daggers, while Princess Noub-Hotep showed bone shapes linked to drawing a bow. These details mean the princesses likely practiced archery and martial arts from a young age.

**Impact** – This discovery challenges the old idea that royal women lived quiet, decorative lives. Instead, these princesses were active and skilled fighters who faced real dangers, including injuries like broken ribs and fractures. Their royal status allowed them access to good medical care, showing both their strength and resilience.

**What's next step** – Scientists will continue studying more mummies and ancient remains to better understand women’s roles in history. This research could change how we think about gender and power in ancient societies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Bone clues from ancient Egyptian princesses show they were trained fighters, revealing a more active and powerful role for royal women than previously thought.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les princesses égyptiennes anciennes formées comme guerrières, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les princesses égyptiennes antiques étaient souvent enterrées avec des armes comme des arcs et des poignards, mais on ne savait pas si elles les utilisaient réellement ou si c’était juste symbolique.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié six momies royales vieilles d’environ 3800 ans, dont cinq femmes, supposées être des filles du pharaon Amenemhat II. Ils ont découvert des modifications dans les os des bras et des mains qui suggèrent que ces femmes utilisaient régulièrement des armes. Par exemple, la princesse Ita avait des os de l’avant-bras renforcés liés à la tenue de poignards, tandis que la princesse Noub-Hotep présentait des os adaptés à la tension d’un arc. Cela signifie qu’elles s’entraînaient probablement au tir à l’arc et aux arts martiaux dès leur jeunesse.

**Impact** – Cette découverte remet en question l’idée que les femmes royales vivaient une vie calme et décorative. Au contraire, ces princesses étaient actives et compétentes au combat, affrontant même des blessures graves. Leur statut leur permettait d’accéder à de bons soins médicaux, montrant leur force et leur résilience.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont continuer à étudier d’autres momies et restes anciens pour mieux comprendre le rôle des femmes dans l’histoire. Ces recherches pourraient changer notre vision du genre et du pouvoir dans les sociétés anciennes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des indices sur les os des princesses égyptiennes anciennes montrent qu’elles étaient formées au combat, révélant un rôle plus actif et puissant que ce qu’on pensait auparavant.
articles.personality_title_es: Princesas del antiguo Egipto probablemente entrenadas como guerreras, revela estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las princesas del antiguo Egipto a menudo eran enterradas con armas como arcos y dagas, pero no estaba claro si realmente las usaban o si solo eran símbolos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron seis momias reales de hace unos 3800 años, cinco de ellas mujeres que se cree eran hijas del faraón Amenemhat II. Encontraron cambios en los huesos de sus brazos y manos que indican que usaban armas con frecuencia. Por ejemplo, la princesa Ita tenía huesos fuertes en el antebrazo relacionados con sostener dagas, y la princesa Noub-Hotep tenía huesos adaptados para tensar un arco. Esto sugiere que entrenaban en tiro con arco y artes marciales desde jóvenes.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo desafía la idea de que las mujeres reales llevaban vidas tranquilas y decorativas. Estas princesas eran activas y hábiles en combate, enfrentándose a lesiones graves. Su rango les daba acceso a buen cuidado médico, mostrando su fuerza y resistencia.

**Qué sigue** – Los científicos continuarán estudiando más momias y restos antiguos para entender mejor el papel de las mujeres en la historia. Esta investigación podría cambiar cómo vemos el género y el poder en sociedades antiguas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las señales en los huesos de princesas egipcias antiguas muestran que fueron entrenadas como guerreras, revelando un papel más activo y poderoso del que se pensaba.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of ancient Egyptian royal artifacts—a richly decorated gold and lapis lazuli dagger and a curved wooden bow with arrows—resting atop textured, weathered stone resembling mummified bones, with subtle shadows suggesting strong, graceful hands holding the weapons, all rendered in natural earth tones and soft light to evoke resilience and active strength.
